current_focus: Treat K=128 only as the initial search anchor, not as a success criterion. The research objective is to find configurations whose FVU is dramatically better than the current K=128 baseline, with a target on the order of halving that baseline FVU before rewarding smaller K or lower cost.
architecture_findings: round 1: A JumpReLU encoder with learnable per-latent thresholds can improve the K=128 quality anchor versus plain TopK by suppressing weak activations before top-k selection, improving feature selectivity and lowering FVU without changing the execution layer. -> crash (fvu=None, observed_fvu=None, k=None, health=crash, termination=identical_to_baseline)

round 2: The new JumpReLU family deserves its first proxy measurement at the K=128 quality anchor; learnable thresholds with smooth gating may suppress weak activations and beat plain TopK FVU before any cost-oriented moves. -> keep (fvu=0.1887873373925686, observed_fvu=0.184100, k=128, health=normal, termination=completed)

round 7: A gated SAE that decouples latent support selection from reconstruction magnitude can beat the current JumpReLU anchor at K=128 by learning a separate sigmoid routing branch while staying initialization-close to plain ReLU top-k. -> crash (fvu=None, observed_fvu=None, k=None, health=crash, termination=identical_to_baseline)
performance_findings: round 3: suspected performance regression for jumprelu (tps=16601.650675, baseline_ratio=0.200000, termination=throughput_too_low)

failure_patterns.pattern: A JumpReLU encoder with learnable per-latent thresholds can improve the K=128 quality anchor versus plain TopK by suppressing weak activations before top-k selection, improving feature selectivity and lowering FVU without changing the execution layer.

JumpReLU can reduce FVU further at K=128 by making its gating sharper and more selective; increasing the initial threshold while narrowing the bandwidth should suppress weak activations more aggressively and improve reconstruction quality.

A gated SAE that decouples latent support selection from reconstruction magnitude can beat the current JumpReLU anchor at K=128 by learning a separate sigmoid routing branch while staying initialization-close to plain ReLU top-k.

jumprelu.design_hypothesis: JumpReLU at K=128 may still be under-updating relative to its new 1.6e-3 optimum, and increasing the signum learning rate to 3.2e-3 could further reduce FVU beyond the current 0.1724 full frontier.
known_issues: round 3: suspected performance regression for jumprelu (tps=16601.650675, baseline_ratio=0.200000, termination=throughput_too_low)
next_steps: If LR=3.2e-3 improves proxy FVU, promote to full and continue local LR refinement around the new optimum.
If LR=3.2e-3 is worse than 1.6e-3, treat 1.6e-3 as the current LR anchor and begin auxk_alpha sweeps at K=128, starting with 0.
If LR=3.2e-3 is unstable rather than merely worse, stop upward LR exploration and shift to auxk_alpha or expansion-factor sweeps on the 1.6e-3 baseline.

gated.design_hypothesis: A gated SAE that decouples latent support selection from reconstruction magnitude can beat the current JumpReLU anchor at K=128 by learning a separate sigmoid routing branch while staying initialization-close to plain ReLU top-k.
next_steps: If gated shows better proxy FVU at acceptable throughput, stabilize the family by exposing and tuning gate temperature/init strength in a follow-up.
If gated quality is promising but throughput regresses, keep the family alive and do a performance-focused follow-up rather than discarding the architecture.
If gated fails to improve proxy FVU, move on to a different routing-focused family instead of more JumpReLU-local sweeps.

recent_rounds.hypothesis: A JumpReLU encoder with learnable per-latent thresholds can improve the K=128 quality anchor versus plain TopK by suppressing weak activations before top-k selection, improving feature selectivity and lowering FVU without changing the execution layer.

The new JumpReLU family deserves its first proxy measurement at the K=128 quality anchor; learnable thresholds with smooth gating may suppress weak activations and beat plain TopK FVU before any cost-oriented moves.

JumpReLU can reduce FVU further at K=128 by making its gating sharper and more selective; increasing the initial threshold while narrowing the bandwidth should suppress weak activations more aggressively and improve reconstruction quality.

A gated SAE that decouples latent support selection from reconstruction magnitude can beat the current JumpReLU anchor at K=128 by learning a separate sigmoid routing branch while staying initialization-close to plain ReLU top-k.

A softer JumpReLU gate may recover stable throughput and improve proxy FVU versus the failed sharp-gating attempt by widening the bandwidth around the current best K=128 anchor without changing architecture or optimizer.

The current best JumpReLU configuration may retain enough selectivity at K=64 to improve cost meaningfully while staying close enough to the K=128 quality regime to identify whether this family has a viable smaller-K path.
JumpReLU may still deliver a usable smaller-K regime at K=32, and measuring that directly is the highest-value next step now that K=64 stayed stable enough to earn a full run.

JumpReLU at the K=128 quality anchor may be undertrained at the current signum learning rate, and lowering LR to 4e-4 could improve optimization stability enough to beat the current 0.1888 full-FVU baseline.

The current JumpReLU K=128 baseline may benefit from more aggressive signum updates, and increasing LR to 1.6e-3 could improve optimization enough to beat both the failed 4e-4 proxy and the standing 0.1888 full-FVU anchor.
JumpReLU at K=128 may still be under-updating relative to its new 1.6e-3 optimum, and increasing the signum learning rate to 3.2e-3 could further reduce FVU beyond the current 0.1724 full frontier.

recent_insights: JumpReLU established the current K=128 full frontier at FVU 0.1887873373925686, so the next round should tune its threshold dynamics rather than reduce K.
Round 3 tests a sharper JumpReLU gate via higher init threshold and lower bandwidth as the first stabilization pass.

round 3: JumpReLU can reduce FVU further at K=128 by making its gating sharper and more selective; increasing the initial threshold while narrowing the bandwidth should suppress weak activations more aggressively and improve reconstruction quality. -> discard (fvu=0.3432992845773697, observed_fvu=0.340764, k=128, health=perf_regression, termination=throughput_too_low)

round 6: agent invocation failed after retries: Agent invocation failed after 4 attempts: codex exec failed: see /root/sparsify-ascend/research/history/logs/agent_round_0006.stdout.log
Round 7: variable isolation warning: Multiple primary variables changed simultaneously: code_edit, architecture
Added a new `gated` SAE family under `sparsify/` with a second encoder branch that produces sigmoid gates for support selection.
The gate branch is initialized with zero weights and a positive bias so the prototype starts close to plain ReLU top-k rather than an aggressively sparse regime.
Current execution-layer hooks do not expose gated-specific env knobs, so this first prototype relies on default `gated_temperature=1.0` and `gated_init_logit=2.0`.

round 7: A gated SAE that decouples latent support selection from reconstruction magnitude can beat the current JumpReLU anchor at K=128 by learning a separate sigmoid routing branch while staying initialization-close to plain ReLU top-k. -> crash (fvu=None, observed_fvu=None, k=None, health=crash, termination=identical_to_baseline)
Round 8 used crash-recovery param-only mode and stayed on the best working JumpReLU K=128 anchor.
Tested a softer JumpReLU gate by widening bandwidth from 0.1 to 0.15 while keeping the threshold at 0.15 to separate stability effects from architecture changes.
This round is intended to check whether the round-3 regression came from overly sharp gating rather than from JumpReLU itself.

round 8: A softer JumpReLU gate may recover stable throughput and improve proxy FVU versus the failed sharp-gating attempt by widening the bandwidth around the current best K=128 anchor without changing architecture or optimizer. -> archive (fvu=0.18926890939474106, observed_fvu=0.184603, k=128, health=normal, termination=completed)
Round 9: variable isolation warning: Multiple primary variables changed simultaneously: architecture, k
Round 9 starts the required smaller-K exploration on the current best working family rather than introducing another architecture.
The K=64 test keeps the best known JumpReLU anchor settings fixed so any movement is attributable primarily to lower sparsity budget.
This round is about establishing whether JumpReLU has a credible smaller-K path before continuing systematic LR, auxk, or expansion sweeps.

round 9: The current best JumpReLU configuration may retain enough selectivity at K=64 to improve cost meaningfully while staying close enough to the K=128 quality regime to identify whether this family has a viable smaller-K path. -> keep (fvu=0.23653452284634113, observed_fvu=0.230266, k=64, health=normal, termination=completed)
Round 10: variable isolation warning: Multiple primary variables changed simultaneously: architecture, k
Round 10 completes the required K exploration ladder by testing the best working JumpReLU setup at K=32.
K=64 was viable enough to warrant a full run, so K=32 is now the cleanest remaining smaller-K measurement before more parameter sweeps.
This round keeps all non-K settings fixed to preserve interpretability of the K scaling curve within JumpReLU.

round 10: JumpReLU may still deliver a usable smaller-K regime at K=32, and measuring that directly is the highest-value next step now that K=64 stayed stable enough to earn a full run. -> keep (fvu=0.28750021010637283, observed_fvu=0.278027, k=32, health=normal, termination=completed)
Round 11: variable isolation warning: Multiple primary variables changed simultaneously: architecture, lr
Completed the required K exploration ladder: K=64 and K=32 both remained workable but did not beat the K=128 quality anchor.
Round 11 returns to the best-quality JumpReLU K=128 setup and begins the requested single-parameter LR sweep.
Testing LR=4e-4 first probes whether the current baseline is limited by overly aggressive signum updates rather than by model capacity or sparsity budget.

round 11: JumpReLU at the K=128 quality anchor may be undertrained at the current signum learning rate, and lowering LR to 4e-4 could improve optimization stability enough to beat the current 0.1888 full-FVU baseline. -> discard (fvu=0.36220645159482956, observed_fvu=0.359630, k=128, health=normal, termination=completed)
Round 12: variable isolation warning: Multiple primary variables changed simultaneously: architecture, lr
LR=4e-4 was substantially worse than the K=128 JumpReLU baseline in proxy, so lower-LR stabilization is not promising on this family at the anchor setting.
Round 12 tests the opposite LR direction at 1.6e-3 while keeping all other JumpReLU K=128 settings fixed.
This round is intended to determine whether the current baseline is limited by under-aggressive optimization rather than architecture or sparsity budget.

round 12: The current JumpReLU K=128 baseline may benefit from more aggressive signum updates, and increasing LR to 1.6e-3 could improve optimization enough to beat both the failed 4e-4 proxy and the standing 0.1888 full-FVU anchor. -> keep (fvu=0.17238502204418182, observed_fvu=0.167707, k=128, health=normal, termination=completed)
Round 13: variable isolation warning: Multiple primary variables changed simultaneously: architecture, lr
LR=1.6e-3 established a new K=128 full frontier for JumpReLU, confirming that optimization rather than architecture was limiting the previous baseline.
Round 13 continues the same K=128 JumpReLU LR sweep by testing 3.2e-3 as the next higher bracket.
This round is intended to check whether the LR improvement trend extends upward or whether 1.6e-3 is already near the useful limit.

round 13: JumpReLU at K=128 may still be under-updating relative to its new 1.6e-3 optimum, and increasing the signum learning rate to 3.2e-3 could further reduce FVU beyond the current 0.1724 full frontier. -> archive (fvu=0.1714103352278471, observed_fvu=0.167209, k=128, health=normal, termination=completed)

If LR=3.2e-3 is worse than 1.6e-3, treat 1.6e-3 as the current LR anchor and begin auxk_alpha sweeps at K=128, starting with 0.
If LR=3.2e-3 is unstable rather than merely worse, stop upward LR exploration and shift to auxk_alpha or expansion-factor sweeps on the 1.6e-3 baseline.
